chat.author: Will Law
chat.text: very clear for remote

chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: Jana: when in Europe, it's called "football" ;-)

chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: (And I believe American football is called "handegg" here)

chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: Jake "Cassandra" Holland was warning us about this (inability of unicast live delivery to scale) 8 years ago.

chat.author: Alan Frindell
chat.text: Where in the network does unicast hurt the most?  Is it getting to the edge, in access networks, or both?

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: @Alan as suggested by Jana, feel free to ask the Q ;-)

chat.author: Will Law
chat.text: @Alan - it hurts in the last mile, in the pipe to your house.

chat.author: Mike Blanche
chat.text: @Will really, even in the era of FTTH?

chat.author: Mike Blanche
chat.text: i would guess the capacity issue was the bit above that - from the cabinet/head end/fibre aggregation point back to a local/regional POP that can host CDNs etc?

chat.author: Will Law
chat.text: @Mike - f everyone had FFTH, then my statement is incorrect. However that nirvana is some decades away, so we deal with the reality of congested cable/cellular/ADSL last mile networks on a daily basis.

chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: Only 30% of homes in the US are connected by FTTH. Everyone else is on some kind of shared access network (cable, LTE, satellite). Nowadays, even cable networks are typically fiber close to the home, but still have shared loops that span neighborhoods.

chat.author: Leslie Daigle
chat.text: Taking the opportunity to point out that my "last mile" at home is actually 13 miles over the air (fixed LTE).

chat.author: Leslie Daigle
chat.text: And, yes, we have an antenna, and leverage actual terrestrial broadcast :^)

chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: I am wondering if ingress/peering is a problem for smaller providers (i.e., those that don't have their own backbones but need to buy an internet uplink from a higher-tier provider)

chat.author: Sanjay Mishra
chat.text: Agree with Will that unicast still is a bigger issue for the proverbial last mile when you are dealing with linear TV

chat.author: Mike Blanche
chat.text: @Will fair enough. I forget sometimes that Europe has almost 80% FTTH coverage, and the rest is almost all VDSL...

chat.author: Sanjay Mishra
chat.text: Even with FTTH we see unicast adds the burden especially if the said unicast is not served over the local caches

chat.author: Erik Nygren
chat.text: For people here with networks, how many run into problems with scale/capacity on NAT64/MAPT/etc style infrastructure during IPv4-only large events?

chat.author: Craig Taylor
chat.text: One persistent issues with multicast is the content owner to network owner handoff. Existing mcast usage in the home is always a "walled garden"

chat.author: Glenn Deen
chat.text: it's interesting that the move to streaming possibly being coupled with with a jump from SD/HD to UDK, that's a big jump in data volume that comes with the migration.

chat.author: Glenn Deen
chat.text: each jump is roughly 4x data, ignoring compression choices.

chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: Craig: I think that is largely a decision based on the difficulty of guaranteeing quality when your multicast has to compete with arbitrary internet traffic. That's one of the big problems that needs to be solved for multicast delivery over the general internet bearer to be viable for paid content.

chat.author: Will Law
chat.text: I think MOQT is an interesting bridge between MC and unicast. We can make a relay mesh have identical network properties to a multicast network if we like (stream splitting etc) during a large sports event, and then we can also make it behave like cache based delivery system, much like unicast. And it can do both at the same time. The limiting factor in both cases is connection to the household, but that is being addressed by FFTH over time.

chat.author: Glenn Deen
chat.text: lest readers think the access networks are always the problem, if you are on a 200Mb down link you can easily handle multiple HD and UHD unicast streams simulataneously.

chat.author: Craig Taylor
chat.text: I notice the the growth rate of final mile delivery speeds [in public data] has outpaced even 8K bitrate growth for some time.... It would be interesting to see how SPN core network growth relates to this also.

chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: Glenn: It's the shared bottleneck that is the issue in such networks, not the actual last-mile (km? meter?) pipe.

chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: Comcast offers me something like 900 Mbps downstream, but not everyone in my neighborhood could use all of that all of the time

chat.author: Will Law
chat.text: Good point Glenn. IN a netflix study last year, they indicated that the average connection to a Netflix device was 37x the bitrate needed to play. This ratio is only increasing over time.

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: Thanks to the panelist, this was really good !

chat.author: Craig Taylor
chat.text: Chris Needham/Will: Any thoughts on when we saturate the need to grow video quality from the bits/sec pov given the optical limits of the typical human?

chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: The panel was great. A top 10 MOPS agenda item, for sure

chat.author: Craig Taylor
chat.text: Thank you panel!

chat.author: Mike Blanche
chat.text:
great panel thank you.
a relevant article: https://www.analysysmason.com/research/content/articles/bandwidth-overproduction-crisis-rdns0/

chat.author: Will Law
chat.text: @Craig - for 2-D video, UHD at 30mbps is delivering fidelity close to limit of the average human visual system to discern. The challenge will come with multi-view XR/AR solutions. Apple Vision PRo is wanting 40-80Mbps with HEVC. I cna imagine that this rises to several hundred Mbps as quality increases, but eventually when we are sending 8K per eye, the eye becomes a natural limit.

chat.author: Matt Green
chat.text: We're certainly finding that the end users aren't  as driven to upgrade to 4k as they were to upgrade to HD.

chat.author: Mike Blanche
chat.text: most people sit too far from a TV to benefit from 4K - https://i.rtings.com/images/optimal-viewing-distance-television-graph-size.png

chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: "You're gonna need a bigger TV"

chat.author: Leslie Daigle
chat.text: A lot depends on the definition of "worth it", I think

chat.author: Kyle Rose
chat.text: Anecdotally, I find UHD looks better than HD in my viewing space (an 80" TV at about 10 feet), but HDR is far more important than the bump in pixel count.

chat.author: Mike Blanche
chat.text: i think that graph is based on what the eye can resolve https://www.rtings.com/tv/reviews/by-size/size-to-distance-relationship
